questions.q: The Ramachandran plot is a key diagram in which field of science?
questions.explanation: The Ramachandran plot is a foundational tool in structural biology for analysing protein conformations.

questions.q: After whom is the Ramachandran plot named?
questions.explanation: The plot is named after the Indian physicist and biophysicist Gopalasamudram Narayana Ramachandran.

questions.q: G. N. Ramachandran was a scientist from which country?
questions.explanation: G. N. Ramachandran was an Indian scientist, celebrated as a pioneer of molecular biophysics in India.

questions.q: What kind of molecule does the Ramachandran plot primarily describe?
questions.explanation: The plot maps the allowed conformations of the backbone of protein molecules.

questions.q: The Ramachandran plot maps the allowed twists of what part of a protein?
questions.explanation: The plot describes the allowed rotations of a protein's polypeptide backbone.

questions.q: Proteins are built from chains of which building blocks, whose arrangement the plot studies?
questions.explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids, and the plot examines the backbone geometry of these chains.

questions.q: What is the Ramachandran plot in form?
questions.explanation: It is a two-dimensional plot of backbone dihedral angles used to visualise allowed conformations.

questions.q: The Ramachandran plot helps determine which conformations of a protein are which?
questions.explanation: It identifies which backbone angle combinations are sterically allowed versus disallowed due to atomic clashes.

questions.q: G. N. Ramachandran is best known as a pioneer in India of which discipline?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran is regarded as a founder of molecular biophysics and crystallography research in India.

questions.q: The Ramachandran plot is widely used today to check the quality of what?
questions.explanation: Crystallographers routinely use the plot to validate that experimentally determined protein structures are geometrically reasonable.

questions.q: The two backbone dihedral angles plotted on the Ramachandran plot are commonly denoted by which Greek letters?
questions.explanation: The axes of the plot are the phi (φ) and psi (ψ) dihedral angles of the polypeptide backbone.

questions.q: The angles phi and psi describe rotation around bonds on either side of which backbone atom?
questions.explanation: Phi and psi are the rotations about the N–Cα and Cα–C bonds flanking the alpha-carbon of each residue.

questions.q: The Ramachandran plot was first introduced in a landmark paper published in approximately which year?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran, Ramakrishnan and Sasisekharan introduced the conformational map in their 1963 paper.

questions.q: At which Indian institution did G. N. Ramachandran build his renowned group while developing the plot?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran led influential biophysics research at the University of Madras during the early 1960s.

questions.q: Two co-authors who helped develop the original Ramachandran plot were:
questions.explanation: The 1963 plot was developed by Ramachandran together with C. Ramakrishnan and V. Sasisekharan.

questions.q: Before his work on protein conformation, Ramachandran was famous for proposing the molecular structure of which protein?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran, with Gopinath Kartha, proposed the triple-helical structure of collagen in the 1950s.

questions.q: On the Ramachandran plot, one prominent allowed region corresponds to which common secondary structure?
questions.explanation: Right-handed alpha-helices cluster in a distinct allowed region of the phi-psi map.

questions.q: Besides the alpha-helix region, another major allowed cluster on the plot corresponds to which structure?
questions.explanation: Extended beta-sheet conformations occupy a separate large allowed region of the Ramachandran plot.

questions.q: Which amino acid is exceptional on the Ramachandran plot because its ring restricts its phi angle severely?
questions.explanation: Proline's cyclic side chain locks its phi angle near about minus 60 degrees, giving it a restricted region.

questions.q: Which amino acid can access a much larger area of the Ramachandran plot because it lacks a side chain?
questions.explanation: Glycine has only a hydrogen as its side chain, so it has far less steric hindrance and occupies extra allowed regions.

questions.q: The original allowed and disallowed regions on the plot were determined primarily by considering what?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran and colleagues modelled atoms as hard spheres and ruled out angle combinations causing atomic overlaps.

questions.q: In modern crystallography, a residue falling in a disallowed region of the plot usually signals what?
options: A possible modelling error or unusual strained conformation
questions.explanation: Outliers in disallowed regions typically flag potential errors in a model, though some are genuine strained residues.

questions.q: The third backbone dihedral angle, omega (ω), describing the peptide bond, is usually fixed near which value?
questions.explanation: The peptide bond is planar and predominantly trans, so omega is normally close to 180 degrees, which is why the plot focuses on phi and psi.

questions.q: Ramachandran founded the Molecular Biophysics Unit at which Indian institution in the early 1970s?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran established the Molecular Biophysics Unit at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc), Bangalore.

questions.q: The collagen triple-helix model that preceded the plot was based on X-ray diffraction data from which tissue?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran's collagen work used X-ray fibre diffraction patterns from collagen-rich connective tissue such as tendon.

questions.q: In addition to fully allowed regions, the classic Ramachandran plot also marks regions that are which?
options: Outer (partially) allowed under relaxed contact criteria
questions.explanation: The plot distinguishes 'fully allowed' from larger 'partially allowed' (outer-limit) regions defined by slightly relaxed contact distances.

questions.q: Glycine's special status arises because its side chain is which atom?
questions.explanation: Glycine's side chain is a single hydrogen, minimising steric clashes and broadening its allowed conformational space.

questions.q: G. N. Ramachandran earned his doctoral training in X-ray crystallography under which Nobel laureate at Cambridge?
questions.explanation: Ramachandran worked at Cambridge under Sir Lawrence Bragg, a pioneer of X-ray crystallography and Nobel laureate.

questions.q: Which statement best describes a residue plotted in the broad alpha-helical basin of the plot?
options: It has positive phi and positive psi near 180
It has roughly phi near minus 60 and psi near minus 45
It has phi and psi both near zero
questions.explanation: Right-handed alpha-helical residues typically have phi about minus 60 degrees and psi about minus 45 degrees.

questions.q: Why is the Ramachandran plot considered a cornerstone validation tool rather than merely descriptive?
options: It objectively flags backbone geometry that violates known steric limits
questions.explanation: Because the allowed regions reflect physical steric limits, deviations provide an objective check on the correctness of a structural model.
